children.text: Talking and they- I think that’s when I started getting very, very angry. I was the editor of the newspaper. I used to write some pretty controversial, "supposedly controversial," editorials. I was protesting the Vietnam War at that time. That was in ’67-’68. You know, I was advocating for women priests, for priests to get married. All of this was coming out in the the late sixties. In ’68. So I was taking those stands. Luckily for me, my advisor, was another nun, Sister Margret, her name was Sister Subina at that time, bless her heart. May she rest in peace because I loved that nun. Because she kinda defended me and my rights as an editor and to speak my mind. Just put a “in no way does it reflect the opinions”- you know what they do now? She used to make me do that-“In no way does this reflect the opinions of the School of Little Flower.” You know, I was, "okay, I’ll do that."
I read a story about you that a nun gave you a book to read. The autobiography of Malcolm X?

Ah yes, that was in high school. That was Sister Sabina, Sister Margaret. Well, because I was in trouble a lot in high school. I was in trouble all my school life. You know, I think if I would have been one of today's kids, I would have been classified as ADHD or something like that. My brother, my brother next to me, Johnny because we were always halandos. Anyway, I was always in trouble in high school also. Anyway so, Sister Augusta, the principal, used to put me to help mister- Sergio’s father- I wanna remember his name, Maturino, Mr. Maturino clean the school- dust the walls, clean the windows, polish the floor, wash the dishes from the cafeteria, do this, do that- those were my punishments. And Sister Sabina went in and said “You know, it doesn’t seem to be working. Whatever punishment you are giving her does not seem to be working. Why don’t you let me handle her?” You know, and I imagine that’s what happened because all of the sudden, Sister Augusta – she was mean, man- she just said “You are not coming to me anymore, you're coming to Sister- After school, you just go straight over there.” So she said, “Oh, meet me in the library.” And I would meet her in the library. And then uh, I started reading some stuff she would give me to read. And here she comes with this, you know they had those big habits, she had these deep pockets. And she goes "I got something for you." She gave it to me on the weekend. I’ll never forget that. She said take this home, you read it, and then you tell me what you think. That was the first book- the autobiography of Malcom X- I still have that book. Here it is, it's falling apart man, but I still have it. WOW! It was just a big wow.

Then she gave me Catcher in the Rye by J. D. Salinger. She gave me The Jungle. She gave me all these angry books (laughter). At that time he was Leroy Jones, now he is Amari- The Iceman Cometh. I read all these books. She was like “what do you think?” “Why don’t you write something about it?” That is when I started writing. I wrote my very first research paper was on African American literature and Langston Hughes. I did the Harlem Renaissance. And it was awesome. That is how kind of she got me away from all the travesuras that I used to do. It got to the point I did not need to be punished because I was there already. I was already there. You know, she’s the one that told me “you have a gift.”
(Phone Ringing). "Uh, what was I telling you?" Uh, this one teacher, who impacted you.

Oh Sister, Sister- Actually when I did my thesis, I dedicated- I wrote about her. And told people about the books that she- they were on the banned- and later on I found out. The were on the- How she got them I don't know! And she gave them to me! And they were on the list of banned books. All those books I just mentioned. She's like, you know “Read this. Read this. What do you think?” She was from Ireland. She did not have the same, which was incredible. Because here's this nun. I think up until then all my experiences with nuns were negative. Until- and I take that back. I had some positive experiences with like Sister Josephine. She would kinda defend me- “traviesa, what do you expect?” I mean I was a traviesa. So. (Looks towards interviewer) What happened? "Oh sometimes, we ask the questions and once and-" So anyway. So, she treated me kindly and respectfully and she encouraged me to run for the editor of the newspaper at the high school. And I won. I ran and I won. By one vote, but I won. So I used to use that editorial page as my voice to protest. That was high school.

Malcom X was a Black Nationalist leader who was active during the Civil Rights Movement until his assasination in 1965. He advocated for the Islamic faith within the Black community in the United States. "The Autobiography of Malcom X" was written by himself and American journalist Alex Haley.
